_comment: Manual patches for source-data issues found during quality audit. Applied by xlsx_to_json.py after sheet extraction.

co_sql_305-0275.reason: source had only two comment lines as the 'solution'; rewrote as a concrete, runnable demonstration of the dynamic-SQL pattern using SQLite JSON1 (json_extract). Also broadened the schema and difficulty.
co_sql_305-0275.question:
A products catalog stores category-specific attributes as a JSON blob in `attribute_json`. Different categories have different keys:

  • phones — screen_size, ram_gb, storage_gb
  • books  — author, pages, isbn
  • shirts — size, color, fabric

Write a SQL query that returns each product with its category-relevant primary and secondary attributes EXTRACTED into named columns. This is the 'dynamic SQL simulation' pattern: in a real app you'd code-generate per-category SELECTs; in pure SQL you simulate the same effect with CASE + json_extract().

Expected output (six rows ordered by category, product_id):

  product_id | category | primary_attr      | secondary_attr
  3          | book     | Ursula K. Le Guin | 254
  4          | book     | N.K. Jemisin      | 449
  1          | phone    | 6.1               | 8
  2          | phone    | 6.7               | 12
  5          | shirt    | M                 | navy
  6          | shirt    | L                 | olive
co_sql_305-0275.solution:
-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Setup — products with category-specific JSON attributes │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS products;
CREATE TABLE products (
    product_id     INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    category       TEXT NOT NULL,
    attribute_json TEXT NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO products VALUES
  -- Phones — screen_size, ram_gb, storage_gb
  (1, 'phone', '{"screen_size": 6.1, "ram_gb": 8,  "storage_gb": 128}'),
  (2, 'phone', '{"screen_size": 6.7, "ram_gb": 12, "storage_gb": 256}'),
  -- Books — author, pages, isbn
  (3, 'book',  '{"author": "Ursula K. Le Guin", "pages": 254, "isbn": "9780441478125"}'),
  (4, 'book',  '{"author": "N.K. Jemisin",      "pages": 449, "isbn": "9780316229296"}'),
  -- Shirts — size, color, fabric
  (5, 'shirt', '{"size": "M", "color": "navy",  "fabric": "cotton"}'),
  (6, 'shirt', '{"size": "L", "color": "olive", "fabric": "cotton-blend"}');

-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Solution — CASE picks the right JSON path per category │
-- │                                                          │
-- │  This simulates 'dynamic SQL' in pure SQL: the column   │
-- │  selection logic that an app would normally code-       │
-- │  generate is expressed inline with json_extract() +     │
-- │  CASE. Works in SQLite (JSON1), Postgres (->>), MySQL   │
-- │  (->>), Snowflake (:), BigQuery (JSON_VALUE) — same     │
-- │  pattern, dialect-specific operators.                   │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
SELECT
    product_id,
    category,
    CASE category
        WHEN 'phone' THEN json_extract(attribute_json, '$.screen_size')
        WHEN 'book'  THEN json_extract(attribute_json, '$.author')
        WHEN 'shirt' THEN json_extract(attribute_json, '$.size')
    END AS primary_attr,
    CASE category
        WHEN 'phone' THEN json_extract(attribute_json, '$.ram_gb')
        WHEN 'book'  THEN json_extract(attribute_json, '$.pages')
        WHEN 'shirt' THEN json_extract(attribute_json, '$.color')
    END AS secondary_attr
FROM products
ORDER BY category, product_id;

-- ╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
-- │  Variant — when you actually want one wide row per      │
-- │  category with EVERY possible attribute as its own      │
-- │  column, code-generate a UNION ALL per category in the  │
-- │  app layer. Conceptual sketch:                          │
-- ╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
--   SELECT product_id,
--          json_extract(attribute_json, '$.screen_size')  AS screen_size,
--          json_extract(attribute_json, '$.ram_gb')       AS ram_gb,
--          json_extract(attribute_json, '$.storage_gb')   AS storage_gb,
--          NULL AS author, NULL AS pages, NULL AS isbn,
--          NULL AS size,   NULL AS color, NULL AS fabric
--   FROM products WHERE category = 'phone'
--   UNION ALL
--   SELECT product_id, NULL, NULL, NULL,
--          json_extract(attribute_json, '$.author'),
--          json_extract(attribute_json, '$.pages'),
--          json_extract(attribute_json, '$.isbn'),
--          NULL, NULL, NULL
--   FROM products WHERE category = 'book'
--   UNION ALL …  -- one branch per category

co_sql_305-0108.reason: source had only SQL-comment pseudocode; rewrote as runnable Python
co_sql_305-0108.solution:
import sqlite3, csv

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.executescript("""
CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER, name TEXT, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO t VALUES (1,'A',10.5),(2,'B',7.25),(3,'C',99.0);
""")
rows = con.execute('SELECT * FROM t').fetchall()
cols = [d[0] for d in con.execute('SELECT * FROM t').description]

# Write to CSV (in-memory demo).
import io
buf = io.StringIO()
w = csv.writer(buf); w.writerow(cols); w.writerows(rows)
print(buf.getvalue())

co_sql_305-0210.reason: source had only SQL-comment pseudocode; rewrote as runnable Python
co_sql_305-0210.solution:
import sqlite3

# Pretend each call to fetch_page() hits a paginated API.
# It returns up to `page_size` rows, or [] when exhausted.
_pages = [
    [(1, 'a'), (2, 'b')],
    [(3, 'c')],
    [],  # end-of-stream marker
]
def fetch_page(page):
    return _pages[page] if page < len(_pages) else []

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.execute('CREATE TABLE items (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT)')

page = 0
while True:
    batch = fetch_page(page)
    if not batch: break
    con.executemany('INSERT INTO items VALUES (?, ?)', batch)
    page += 1
con.commit()
print(con.execute('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM items').fetchone()[0], 'rows loaded')

co_sql_305-0211.reason: source had only SQL-comment pseudocode; rewrote as runnable Python (no pandas dep needed for the demo)
co_sql_305-0211.solution:
import sqlite3, csv, io

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.executescript("""
CREATE TABLE customers (id INTEGER, name TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders    (id INTEGER, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO customers VALUES (1,'Alex'),(2,'Bailey');
INSERT INTO orders    VALUES (10,1,42.0),(11,1,8.0),(12,2,99.5);
""")

q = '''
  SELECT c.id, c.name, SUM(o.amount) AS total
  FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
  GROUP BY c.id, c.name
  ORDER BY total DESC
'''
rows = con.execute(q).fetchall()
cols = [d[0] for d in con.execute(q).description]

buf = io.StringIO()
w = csv.writer(buf); w.writerow(cols); w.writerows(rows)
print(buf.getvalue())

co_sql_305-0212.reason: source had only SQL-comment pseudocode; rewrote as runnable Python
co_sql_305-0212.solution:
import sqlite3, csv, io

CSV_TEXT = 'id,name,price\n1,widget,3.5\n2,gizmo,9.0\n3,gadget,12.75\n'

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.execute('CREATE TABLE products (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, price REAL)')

reader = csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(CSV_TEXT))
con.executemany(
    'INSERT INTO products (id, name, price) VALUES (:id, :name, :price)',
    [{'id': int(r['id']), 'name': r['name'], 'price': float(r['price'])} for r in reader],
)
con.commit()
print(con.execute('SELECT * FROM products').fetchall())

co_sql_305-0213.reason: source had only SQL-comment pseudocode; rewrote as runnable Python
co_sql_305-0213.solution:
import sqlite3, csv, io
from collections import defaultdict

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.executescript("""
CREATE TABLE sales (region TEXT, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO sales VALUES ('NA',100),('NA',50),('APAC',75),('EMEA',125),('APAC',25);
""")

rows = con.execute('SELECT region, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales GROUP BY region ORDER BY total DESC').fetchall()

# Pandas+Excel would be: pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=['region','total']).to_excel('out.xlsx', index=False)
# Equivalent CSV output for a runnable demo:
buf = io.StringIO()
w = csv.writer(buf); w.writerow(['region','total']); w.writerows(rows)
print(buf.getvalue())

co_sql_305-0214.reason: source had only SQL-comment pseudocode; rewrote as runnable Python (UPSERT pattern)
co_sql_305-0214.solution:
import sqlite3, csv, io

CSV_TEXT = 'product_id,name,price\n1,widget,3.50\n2,gizmo,9.00\n3,gadget,12.75\n'

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.execute('''CREATE TABLE products (
    product_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    name TEXT,
    price REAL
)''')
con.execute('INSERT INTO products VALUES (1, "old-widget", 1.00)')

for row in csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(CSV_TEXT)):
    con.execute('''
        INSERT INTO products (product_id, name, price)
        VALUES (?, ?, ?)
        ON CONFLICT(product_id) DO UPDATE SET
            name  = excluded.name,
            price = excluded.price
    ''', (int(row['product_id']), row['name'], float(row['price'])))
con.commit()
print(con.execute('SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY product_id').fetchall())

co_sql_305-0296.reason: source had only SQL-comment pseudocode; rewrote as runnable Python
co_sql_305-0296.solution:
from collections import defaultdict

# Three CSV-style sources to combine
src_a = [{'region': 'NA',   'amount': 100}, {'region': 'NA',   'amount':  50}]
src_b = [{'region': 'EMEA', 'amount':  75}, {'region': 'APAC', 'amount': 125}]
src_c = [{'region': 'APAC', 'amount':  25}, {'region': 'NA',   'amount':  10}]

totals = defaultdict(float)
for row in (*src_a, *src_b, *src_c):
    totals[row['region']] += row['amount']

report = sorted(totals.items(), key=lambda kv: -kv[1])
for region, total in report:
    print(f'{region:6s} {total:8.2f}')

co_sql_305-0297.reason: source had only SQL-comment pseudocode; rewrote as runnable Python (parquet replaced with sqlite-only demo since pyarrow may not be loaded)
co_sql_305-0297.solution:
import sqlite3, json, datetime

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.executescript("""
CREATE TABLE t (id INTEGER, payload TEXT, created_at TEXT);
INSERT INTO t VALUES (1,'alpha','2025-01-01'), (2,'beta','2025-01-02');
""")

rows = con.execute('SELECT * FROM t').fetchall()
cols = [d[0] for d in con.execute('SELECT * FROM t').description]

# In production: df = pd.read_sql('SELECT * FROM t', con)
#                df.to_parquet('out.parquet', engine='pyarrow', compression='snappy')
# For an in-browser demo we serialize to a metadata-rich JSON envelope.
envelope = {
    'schema': cols,
    'row_count': len(rows),
    'exported_at': datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + 'Z',
    'rows': [dict(zip(cols, r)) for r in rows],
}
print(json.dumps(envelope, indent=2))

python_175-0059.reason: source workbook truncated the conditional expression after 'else'
python_175-0059.solution:
from collections import Counter

def minWindow(s, t):
    if not s or not t: return ""
    need = Counter(t); have = {}
    have_cnt, need_cnt = 0, len(need)
    res, res_len = [-1, -1], float('inf')
    l = 0
    for r, c in enumerate(s):
        have[c] = have.get(c, 0) + 1
        if c in need and have[c] == need[c]: have_cnt += 1
        while have_cnt == need_cnt:
            if r - l + 1 < res_len:
                res, res_len = [l, r], r - l + 1
            have[s[l]] -= 1
            if s[l] in need and have[s[l]] < need[s[l]]: have_cnt -= 1
            l += 1
    return s[res[0]:res[1] + 1] if res_len < float('inf') else ""

community_solved-0046.reason: source solution uses SQL Server UNPIVOT, which the in-browser SQLite engine doesn't support. Rewrote with the portable UNION ALL pattern (the same approach already shown as 'method 3' in the source) so it runs in the playground. UNPIVOT is shown as a reference variant in a comment.
community_solved-0046.solution:
-- Question: turn the columns English, Maths, and Science into rows so each
-- student-subject pair appears as its own row with a single Marks column.
--
-- The portable approach — works in SQLite, Postgres, MySQL, Snowflake — is
-- a UNION ALL: emit one SELECT per source column, hard-coding the subject
-- name as a literal.

SELECT StudentName, 'English' AS Subject, English AS Marks
FROM student_info
UNION ALL
SELECT StudentName, 'Maths'   AS Subject, Maths   AS Marks
FROM student_info
UNION ALL
SELECT StudentName, 'Science' AS Subject, Science AS Marks
FROM student_info
ORDER BY StudentName, Subject;

-- Reference variant — SQL Server / Oracle UNPIVOT (won't run in SQLite):
--
--   SELECT StudentName, Subject, Marks
--   FROM (SELECT StudentName, English, Maths, Science FROM student_info) p
--   UNPIVOT (Marks FOR Subject IN (English, Maths, Science)) u;

co_sql_305-0003.reason: SELECT labelled buckets with CASE WHEN COUNT(*) = 1 but GROUP BY grouped on CASE WHEN cnt = 1; COUNT(*) counts grouped customer rows, not order count, so every bucket was mislabelled 'Returning'. Label expression now matches the GROUP BY key.
co_sql_305-0003.solution:
SELECT
  CASE WHEN cnt = 1 THEN 'New' ELSE 'Returning' END AS customer_type,
  COUNT(*) AS customers
FROM (SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) t
GROUP BY CASE WHEN cnt = 1 THEN 'New' ELSE 'Returning' END
ORDER BY customer_type;

co_sql_305-0038.reason: SELECT emitted two output columns both named shift_id; added distinct aliases shift_a / shift_b so object-keyed result consumers don't collapse the pair.
co_sql_305-0038.solution:
SELECT a.shift_id AS shift_a, b.shift_id AS shift_b
FROM shifts a JOIN shifts b
  ON a.employee_id = b.employee_id AND a.shift_id < b.shift_id
 AND a.start_time < b.end_time AND b.start_time < a.end_time;
